stories.title: The Commonwealth Games open in Glasgow today — India sends a 126-athlete squad led by Neeraj Chopra, with Mirabai Chanu carrying the flag
key_points: Opening ceremony tonight at Glasgow's OVO Hydro
126 Indian athletes competing, July 23 to August 2
Neeraj Chopra (javelin) is India's biggest medal hope
Mirabai Chanu is flag bearer, Lovlina Borgohain the baton bearer
stories.background: The Commonwealth Games is a multi-sport event held every four years among countries that were once part of the British Empire — think of it as a smaller, regional version of the Olympics, with around 70 nations and territories taking part. India has historically done well here, especially in weightlifting, wrestling, badminton and boxing, and uses it as a mid-cycle benchmark between Olympic Games. This edition is being hosted in Glasgow, Scotland.
stories.what_happened: The 2026 Commonwealth Games officially open today in Glasgow with a ceremony at the OVO Hydro arena, running through August 2. India is sending a 126-athlete contingent, headlined by two-time Olympic medallist javelin thrower Neeraj Chopra, Tokyo Olympics silver medallist weightlifter Mirabai Chanu (who is India's flag bearer), and boxer Lovlina Borgohain (baton bearer). The ceremony features Scottish musical acts including Tom Walker and KT Tunstall alongside the traditional athletes' parade.
stories.why_it_matters: For a country of over a billion people, Commonwealth medal counts are one of the more visible, tracked measures of India's sporting investment paying off — success here (or failure) shapes public and government conversation about athlete funding ahead of the next Olympics. Chopra in particular carries outsized attention as India's first individual Olympic gold medallist in athletics.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong Indian performances typically translate into a bump in sponsorship interest and government sports funding announcements; a disappointing showing usually triggers scrutiny of India's sports administration, similar to debates that follow after other major multi-sport events.
stories.why_now: The Games open today, July 23, and run for eleven days, so the ceremony is the natural news hook.
stories.watch_next: Neeraj Chopra's javelin final and India's medal tally build-up over the next 11 days.
stories.the_lesson: Multi-sport events like the Commonwealth Games function as a public scoreboard for a country's sports development system — the medal count becomes a proxy for a much bigger, harder-to-see set of investments in training and infrastructure.

stories.title: India's state fuel giant HPCL posted a ₹11,526 crore loss — even as its revenue jumped 21%
key_points: HPCL's Q1 loss: ₹11,526 crore standalone (₹12,265 crore consolidated)
Revenue still grew 21% year-on-year to ₹1.45 lakh crore
Cause: it sold petrol, diesel and LPG below cost while crude oil got more expensive
The Iran war's impact on oil prices is the direct trigger
Its refineries ran at 107% of capacity — the refining side alone did fine
stories.background: HPCL (Hindustan Petroleum Corporation) is one of India's three big state-run oil-marketing companies — they refine crude oil into fuel and sell it at the pump. India keeps retail fuel prices relatively stable for consumers even when global crude oil prices spike, which means these companies sometimes have to sell fuel for less than it costs them to make and deliver it. The government generally expects them to absorb short-term losses rather than pass a price shock straight to drivers.
stories.what_happened: HPCL reported a standalone net loss of ₹11,526.41 crore for the quarter ended June 2026, its consolidated loss coming in at ₹12,264.67 crore — a sharp reversal from a ₹4,901.50 crore profit the previous quarter. Despite this, revenue actually grew 21% to ₹1,45,126 crore, and its refineries performed strongly, running at 107% of rated capacity. The loss came entirely from the marketing side: HPCL kept selling petrol, diesel and LPG to consumers at prices that didn't cover its now-higher input costs.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the clearest financial evidence yet of how the US-Iran conflict (and the resulting spike in crude and diesel prices already rippling through this week's news) is directly hitting Indian companies' bottom lines — not just abstractly 'the economy,' but a specific, large, listed company's quarterly numbers. It also illustrates a structural tension in India's fuel-pricing system: state oil companies absorb the shock so pump prices stay politically palatable.
stories.ripple_effects: If the oil-price shock persists, HPCL (and peers like Indian Oil and BPCL) may need government compensation, price hikes, or bond issuances to cover the losses — any of which has knock-on effects, from the fiscal deficit to eventual pump prices for ordinary drivers. Investors in oil-marketing-company stocks now have to price in the risk that geopolitical shocks abroad show up as quarterly losses at home.
stories.why_now: These are the newly-released June-quarter (Q1 FY27) results, reported today, and they land in the same week diesel prices and the Iran war have already been dominant global stories — this is the India-specific financial fallout of that same story.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government announces compensation or a subsidy mechanism for oil-marketing companies, and whether Indian Oil and BPCL report similar losses when their own results land.

key_terms.definition: A company that refines crude oil into usable fuel and sells it through petrol pumps — HPCL, Indian Oil, and BPCL are India's three big state-run OMCs.
The gap between what a fuel company charges consumers and what it costs them to buy crude, refine it, and deliver it — when this margin goes negative, the company loses money on every litre sold.

stories.the_lesson: A company can grow its revenue and still lose a huge amount of money if the price it's allowed to charge doesn't keep pace with its rising costs — revenue growth alone never tells the full story.
stories.market_link: HPCL and other oil-marketing-company stocks (Indian Oil, BPCL) are directly exposed to crude-price swings from the Iran conflict; watch these alongside crude oil futures.

stories.title: Assam's floods just got deadlier — 10 more died overnight, taking the toll to 41
key_points: 41 dead so far this flood season, including 4 children in the last 24 hours alone
6.53 lakh (653,000) people affected across 11 districts
Sivasagar district is worst-hit; Army, NDRF and SDRF have rescued 8,400+ people
Over 3.1 lakh people are sheltering in 487 relief camps
stories.background: Assam, in India's northeast, sits on the floodplain of the Brahmaputra river and floods almost every monsoon (June–September) as rain swells the river and its tributaries. Some years are routine; some are severe. This is a separate flood event from the one that hit Kashmir and Nagaland earlier this week — different rivers, different region, same monsoon system driving both.
stories.what_happened: Assam's disaster management authorities confirmed 10 more deaths in the 24 hours to Thursday, including four children, pushing the season's death toll to 41. The worst-hit district, Sivasagar, alone accounts for over 3.9 lakh of the 6.53 lakh people affected across 11 districts. Rising water levels in the Brahmaputra and its tributary the Disang continue to threaten new areas even as rescue operations continue.
stories.why_it_matters: This is now one of the deadlier flood seasons in Assam in recent years, with the toll still rising day by day rather than plateauing. Beyond the human cost, floods on this scale damage crops, roads, and homes across a state that is already among India's poorer regions — the economic recovery typically takes months after the water recedes.
stories.ripple_effects: Displaced families strain relief infrastructure and local economies for weeks after the immediate crisis. Damaged farmland can push up local food prices and hurt rural incomes for a full growing season. It also puts pressure on the state and central governments to release disaster-relief funds, which becomes a political flashpoint if response is seen as slow.
stories.why_now: The death toll jumped sharply — 10 deaths in a single day — making today's update materially worse than earlier in the week, when the toll stood at 31.
stories.watch_next: Whether the Brahmaputra crosses further danger marks in the coming days, and whether the central government announces additional disaster-relief funding for Assam.

key_terms.definition: The seasonal wind pattern (roughly June–September in India) that brings the bulk of the year's rainfall — vital for agriculture but also the main cause of India's annual flooding.

stories.the_lesson: Flood death tolls in India's northeast are cumulative and can worsen suddenly with a single heavy-rain day — the headline number lags the real situation on the ground, so it's worth checking whether the toll is rising or stabilizing, not just its current level.

stories.title: India-US trade deal talks reach the 'last 1%' of the legal text, with a tariff truce set to expire tomorrow
key_points: Negotiators say the deal's overall framework is largely ready but the final tariff numbers are still unresolved
A temporary 10% US tariff arrangement on Indian goods expires July 24 — tomorrow
Trump has separately set August 1 as a broader deadline for countries to sign or face steep tariffs
India wants a better tariff rate than the one given to Southeast Asian competitors
stories.background: India and the US have spent months negotiating a Bilateral Trade Agreement aimed at cutting tariffs and expanding market access in both directions. Washington had placed a temporary 10% tariff on Indian goods as a placeholder while talks continued, due to lapse this week — after which, without a deal, tariffs could jump sharply, echoing the tariff plan Trump has already floated for other sectors like pharmaceuticals (covered in our earlier reporting).
stories.what_happened: As of today, officials describe the agreement's text as roughly 99% complete — the broad framework covering market access, digital trade, supply-chain cooperation and reduced non-tariff barriers is largely settled — but the single biggest sticking point, the actual tariff rate India will face, remains unresolved. The current temporary 10% tariff arrangement expires tomorrow, July 24, though Trump has set a separate, later deadline of August 1 for several countries including India to finalize deals before facing steeper tariffs.
stories.why_it_matters: Tariffs directly change the price of Indian exports — textiles, pharmaceuticals, electronics, and more — in the US market, India's largest export destination. A worse rate than rivals like Vietnam or Indonesia get would put Indian exporters at a real cost disadvantage overnight.
stories.ripple_effects: Indian equity markets, particularly export-heavy sectors like IT services, pharma, and textiles, are highly sensitive to how this resolves — a bad outcome could extend the Sensex/Nifty pressure we've already reported this week, while a good deal could trigger a relief rally. The rupee, already under pressure, would also likely react to the outcome.
stories.why_now: Tomorrow's deadline for the temporary tariff arrangement forces a decision point this week, even though the harder August 1 deadline gives some additional room.
stories.watch_next: Whether India and the US announce a deal — or another extension — before Friday, July 24.

key_terms.definition: Rules other than tariffs — like licensing requirements or safety standards — that a country uses to restrict imports.

stories.the_lesson: In trade negotiations, the last, smallest-sounding gap (a few percentage points on a tariff line) is often the hardest to close, because it's exactly the part with real money attached for both sides.
stories.market_link: A final deal or its collapse would directly move Nifty IT, pharma and textile stocks, and likely the rupee, given how exposed those sectors are to US tariff exposure.

thread.previously: We reported India and the US racing to close a trade deal before Thursday's temporary tariff deadline, with India still holding out for better terms.
thread.changed: Officials now describe the deal text as roughly 99% complete, with only the final tariff rate unresolved, as the temporary tariff arrangement's expiry (tomorrow, July 24) approaches.

stories.title: Parliament collapses again within 2 minutes as opposition ties Wangchuk's hunger strike to a hospitalized student protester and demands a minister's resignation
key_points: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha both adjourned within 2 minutes of convening at 11am today — Day 4 of chaos
Opposition now demands Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over the 'Chalo Sansad' march crackdown
A young protester injured in that police action is reported on a ventilator at RML Hospital
Delhi HC has separately allowed moving hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk to Medanta Hospital for continuous care
stories.background: India's Parliament has been in near-continuous disruption this week (see our earlier coverage of Day 3) over the NEET exam-paper-leak scandal and the government's handling of student protests demanding accountability. On July 20, a student-led 'Chalo Sansad' ('March to Parliament') protest was met with a police crackdown; opposition leaders including Rahul Gandhi were briefly detained the next day while protesting outside PM Modi's residence over it. Climate activist Sonam Wangchuk has been on a hunger strike over the same NEET issue and was hospitalized mid-strike.
stories.what_happened: Both houses of Parliament convened at 11am today and were adjourned again within two minutes amid uproar, extending the disruption to a fourth day. Reports today add two new, serious details to the story: a young woman injured during the July 20 police crackdown on the 'Chalo Sansad' march is now on a ventilator at RML Hospital in Delhi, and the Delhi High Court has approved shifting Wangchuk — now 25 days into his fast — to Medanta Hospital for round-the-clock care. The opposition is using both developments to demand the resignation of Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan.
stories.why_it_matters: What began as a dispute over an exam scandal has escalated into a story about the human cost of protest policing and a full breakdown of normal parliamentary business. A hospitalized protester and a 25-day hunger strike raise the political stakes well beyond the original NEET complaint — they turn it into a referendum on how the government handles dissent.
stories.ripple_effects: Continued paralysis means pending legislation — including bills the government wanted passed this Monsoon Session — stalls further. It also keeps the story in headlines through the news cycle, pressuring the government either to make concessions (as it partially has, offering Wangchuk compensation and a debate) or risk the situation escalating if the injured protester's condition worsens.
stories.why_now: Today's near-instant adjournment (2 minutes) shows the standoff has hardened rather than cooled since yesterday, and the ventilator/hospital-transfer details are the newest, most serious facts in an already five-day-old story.
stories.watch_next: The injured protester's medical condition, and whether the government offers the written no-punitive-action assurance Wangchuk has demanded as his condition for ending the fast.

key_terms.definition: When a house of Parliament is suspended without setting a date to resume — a step further than a normal daily adjournment, though today's was a same-day pause.

stories.the_lesson: A protest movement's staying power often comes less from its original demand than from a single dramatic, human incident that reframes the story — here, an injury and a hunger strike now carry more political weight than the exam scandal that started it.

thread.previously: We reported the third straight day of chaos, with Lok Sabha adjourned again as the government said it was ready to discuss the NEET leak and blamed Congress for the disruption.
thread.changed: Two serious new facts have emerged: a protester injured in the July 20 crackdown is now on a ventilator, and Wangchuk — now 25 days into his fast — has been cleared by the Delhi High Court to move to Medanta Hospital. The opposition has escalated to demanding the Education Minister's resignation, and today's session was adjourned within 2 minutes.

stories.title: Government offers Sonam Wangchuk compensation and a Parliament debate — but he's still holding out for a written promise
key_points: Ministers Nadda and Singh offered compensation for students who died by suicide over the NEET leak
They also floated a real Parliament debate and 'considering' Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation
Wangchuk says none of that is enough — he wants it in writing that protesting students won't face legal action
His hunger strike is now in its 25th day, with doctors warning of possible organ damage
stories.background: Sonam Wangchuk, a Ladakh-based education reformer and climate activist, has been on hunger strike since June 28 in solidarity with the Gen Z-led protest movement demanding accountability for leaks in the NEET medical-entrance exam, including Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation. He is being treated in the ICU at Delhi's Medanta Hospital. Yesterday, after union ministers visited him in hospital, he wrote to them setting a specific condition for ending his fast: a written guarantee that no legal action would be taken against student protesters.
stories.what_happened: Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh, who visited Wangchuk in the ICU, have now responded to his letter. They told him the government would 'positively consider' compensating the families of students who died by suicide after the NEET leak, hold a genuine discussion in Parliament on exam accountability, and consider Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation. But those are verbal assurances, not the written, binding guarantee Wangchuk specifically asked for — that no student or young protester will face legal or punitive action. He has made clear he won't end his fast without that written commitment. Doctors say he has lost about 21 pounds and warn of a real risk of organ damage the longer the strike continues.
stories.why_it_matters: The gap between what the government is offering (verbal goodwill gestures) and what Wangchuk is demanding (a written, enforceable promise) is exactly the kind of gap that can keep a life-threatening standoff going even when both sides say they want to resolve it.
stories.ripple_effects: If the Centre keeps offering verbal-only concessions, expect Wangchuk's health crisis and the linked Parliament disruptions to continue or worsen; opposition parties are already using the standoff to pressure the government during the Monsoon Session. A written concession, if it comes, would likely end the fast within days and take pressure off Parliament.
stories.why_now: This is the government's first substantive response since Wangchuk issued his specific written condition yesterday — it shows negotiations are happening, but also exactly where they're stuck.
stories.watch_next: Watch for whether the government puts anything in writing in the next few days — that's the single condition standing between Wangchuk and ending his 25-day fast.

key_terms.definition: India's National Eligibility cum Entrance Test — the single exam used to admit students to medical colleges nationwide.

stories.the_lesson: In a standoff, a verbal promise and a written guarantee are not the same thing — protesters who have been burned before often hold out specifically for something legally binding, because spoken assurances can be walked back once public attention moves on.

thread.previously: Wangchuk wrote to the Centre saying he would only end his 24-day hunger strike if given a written assurance that no legal action would be taken against student protesters, a day after ministers visited him in the ICU.
thread.changed: Ministers have now responded with concrete verbal offers — compensation for grieving families, a real Parliament debate, and considering Pradhan's resignation — but Wangchuk says he still needs it in writing before he'll end his fast, now in its 25th day.

stories.title: Commonwealth Games 2026 opens in Glasgow tonight — Mirabai Chanu and Lovlina Borgohain lead India's charge
key_points: Opening ceremony is tonight at Glasgow's OVO Hydro, 10:30pm IST
Olympic medallists Mirabai Chanu (flag bearer) and Lovlina Borgohain (baton bearer) lead India
India's bowls team is already in action today ahead of the ceremony
This is Glasgow's second time hosting the Games, after 2014
stories.background: The Commonwealth Games is a multi-sport event held every four years among athletes from Commonwealth nations — mostly countries with historical ties to the British Empire, including India, the UK, Australia, and dozens of others. Glasgow previously hosted the Games in 2014, and India has traditionally used the event as a proving ground and confidence-builder ahead of the Olympics, especially in weightlifting, boxing, wrestling and shooting.
stories.what_happened: The 2026 Commonwealth Games officially open tonight at Glasgow's OVO Hydro arena, with India's contingent led into the stadium by two Olympic medallists: weightlifter Mirabai Chanu carrying the flag and boxer Lovlina Borgohain carrying the baton. Ahead of the ceremony, India's lawn bowls team was already competing today, with men's singles player Putul Sonowal — ranked 21st in the world — facing Canada's Ryan Bester, and the women's pairs team taking on Malta.
stories.why_it_matters: The Commonwealth Games is one of the few major multi-sport events where India regularly wins a large medal haul, giving athletes in less-funded sports like weightlifting, boxing and wrestling a rare high-profile stage and useful competitive experience heading into the next Olympic cycle.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong Indian showing boosts funding conversations and public attention for the sports involved, while individual breakout performances often shape which athletes get backed for the 2028 Olympics. It's also a moment of national visibility — ceremonies like this are watched by tens of millions back home.
stories.why_now: The Games run on a fixed four-year cycle, and 2026 is Glasgow's turn to host after Victoria, Australia withdrew as host in 2023 over budget concerns, prompting Glasgow to step in on a reduced scale.
stories.watch_next: Watch India's medal tally build over the following two weeks, especially in weightlifting, boxing and wrestling — traditionally India's strongest events at the Commonwealth Games.

key_terms.definition: The athletes chosen to carry their country's flag and the Games' ceremonial baton into the stadium during the opening ceremony — considered a major honor.

stories.the_lesson: Multi-sport events like the Commonwealth Games matter beyond medal counts — for athletes in sports that get little funding or media attention at home, a big stage like this can be the difference between obscurity and the backing needed to reach the Olympics.

stories.title: India and the US race to close a trade deal before Thursday's tariff deadline — India still holding out
key_points: Interim US tariff arrangement (including an extra 10% levy) expires July 24
Deal described as being at the 'last 1%' of legal text, but still unsigned
India wants better tariff terms than Asian rivals get, not just any deal by the deadline
Washington already cut its reciprocal tariff on Indian goods from 25% to 18%
Goal on both sides: $500 billion in two-way trade by 2030 ('Mission 500')
stories.background: Since April 2026, the US has run a 'reciprocal tariff' regime — extra import taxes matched roughly to what other countries charge US goods — with a temporary lower rate for countries actively negotiating trade deals. India has been negotiating a first-phase Bilateral Trade Agreement with the US covering market access, digital trade rules, and non-tariff barriers (rules and standards, rather than tariffs, that make imports harder), aiming to eventually double trade to $500 billion by 2030.
stories.what_happened: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal completed a two-day round of talks with US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, with both sides calling the progress 'substantial.' Washington has already cut its reciprocal tariff on Indian goods from 25% to 18% as talks progressed. But no deal has been signed, and reports describe the legal text as sitting at the 'last 1 per cent.' India is deliberately not rushing to meet the July 24 deadline, instead pushing for tariff terms more competitive than those given to Vietnam, Indonesia and other Asian exporters it competes with.
stories.why_it_matters: If no deal is reached by July 24, the temporary lower tariff rate lapses and Indian exporters could face the higher 'MFN' (most-favoured-nation, i.e., standard) tariff rate on top of the reciprocal tariff — raising costs for Indian goods sold into the US and making them less competitive than rivals who did strike deals.
stories.ripple_effects: A higher tariff wall would hit Indian exporters in textiles, gems and jewellery, and other labour-intensive sectors that compete directly with Vietnam and Indonesia for US shelf space. It also adds to the pressure already squeezing Indian markets this week — a weak rupee, FII (foreign institutional investor) selling, and oil-price shocks from the Iran war — making the rupee and Sensex/Nifty additionally sensitive to any trade-deal headline this week.
stories.why_now: The clock is the story: the current temporary tariff arrangement expires this Thursday (July 24), forcing both sides to either finalize terms or let India fall back to higher default tariffs — with India calculating it's better to wait a bit longer for good terms than rush a bad deal.
stories.watch_next: Watch for a signed announcement (or lack of one) by July 24 — and if the deadline passes without a deal, watch the reciprocal tariff rate that kicks in and how Indian export stocks react.

key_terms.definition: An import tax the US sets roughly matching what it says another country charges US goods — used as both a negotiating tool and a revenue measure.
Rules, standards, or paperwork requirements (not taxes) that make it harder to import goods from another country — often as effective as a tariff at blocking trade.

stories.the_lesson: In trade negotiations, deadlines are often more useful to the side that wants to signal urgency than a hard wall — a country with real leverage (India, a large market and key supplier) can choose to let a deadline pass rather than accept worse terms under time pressure.
stories.market_link: A missed or unfavorable deadline could pressure Nifty-listed exporters (textiles, gems & jewellery, IT services) and add further weakness to the rupee; a deal announcement would likely be a relief rally trigger.

stories.title: Australia name Sam Konstas and two other young batters for a Bangladesh warm-up match
key_points: A 14-player Cricket Australia XI will play Bangladesh in a 3-day warm-up from August 6 in Darwin
Sam Konstas, Campbell Kellaway and Kurtis Patterson are all included
It's a week before the first Test between Australia and Bangladesh
The match won't count as first-class cricket
stories.background: Australia host Bangladesh for a two-Test series in August (Darwin, then Mackay), part of the World Test Championship — the ongoing cycle that decides which two teams reach the WTC final. Before a Test series, home boards often schedule a warm-up match using a second-tier or development squad — the 'CA XI' here — so touring teams get proper match practice and Australia can look at fringe players without risking their senior Test squad.
stories.what_happened: Cricket Australia named a 14-player CA XI, including young batters Sam Konstas and Campbell Kellaway and the more experienced Kurtis Patterson, to play a three-day, red-ball warm-up match against touring Bangladesh, starting August 6 at Marrara Cricket Ground in Darwin — a week before the first Test at the same venue on August 13. The match will be played 13-a-side by agreement between the two boards and won't have official first-class status.
stories.why_it_matters: Selecting Konstas and Kellaway together signals they're being seriously considered as Australia's next Test opening pair, beyond the current senior XI, while Patterson's inclusion shows he remains in the selectors' thinking. It's a look at who Australia's cricket pipeline sees as its future, not just a routine fixture.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong performances here could push these players toward actual Test selection later in the WTC cycle, including tougher assignments coming up in South Africa and England; it also gives Bangladesh's touring squad useful red-ball practice before facing Australia's full-strength Test attack (Pat Cummins, Josh Hazlewood and Nathan Lyon are all returning from injury for the Tests).
stories.why_now: The squad was announced today as part of the buildup to the August Test series, which starts in three weeks.
stories.watch_next: Watch how Konstas and Kellaway perform against the Bangladesh bowling attack, and whether Cricket Australia hints at a settled opening pair for the Test series.

key_terms.definition: The top tier of multi-day domestic cricket, whose statistics count toward official career records — this warm-up match won't count.
A two-year league where every Test-playing nation's results count toward a table, with the top two teams reaching a final.

stories.the_lesson: Warm-up matches that look minor on the surface are often where selectors quietly test their next generation of players before committing them to the real thing.

stories.title: Pakistan's women beat Sri Lanka by 5 wickets, thanks to a 78 from Gull Feroza
key_points: Sri Lanka Women posted 210/9 batting first
Pakistan chased it down with 42 balls to spare, finishing 211/5
Gull Feroza top-scored with 78 off 77 balls and won Player of the Match
Pakistan lead the 3-match ODI series 1-0
stories.background: This is the opening match of Pakistan Women's tour of Sri Lanka, a 3-match ODI series running July 23-28. Both sides have been building their white-ball squads ahead of future ICC events, making bilateral series like this important form guides.
stories.what_happened: At the Mahinda Rajapaksa International Cricket Stadium in Hambantota, Sri Lanka Women batted first and made 210/9 off their 50 overs. In reply, Pakistan Women chased the target down comfortably, reaching 211/5 in 43 overs — a 5-wicket win with 42 balls unused. Opener Gull Feroza was the standout, scoring 78 off 77 balls with 11 fours and reaching her half-century off just 47 balls; she shared a key partnership with Sidra Amin, who made 57. Feroza was named Player of the Match.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a real result, not just a preview — Pakistan have grabbed early momentum in the series with a comfortable, aggressive run-chase, putting Sri Lanka under pressure to respond in the second ODI.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong start builds confidence for Pakistan's middle order ahead of the rest of the series; for Sri Lanka, the loss puts pressure on their top order to find form before the series is out of reach.
stories.why_now: The match was played and completed today as the first fixture of the series.
stories.watch_next: Watch the 2nd ODI, where Sri Lanka will look to level the series.
stories.the_lesson: In a run-chase, a single fast, well-built partnership in the middle overs can settle a match well before the final overs even matter.

stories.title: Kohli and Rohit close in on the top of the ODI rankings — but a Kiwi and an Englishman are still ahead of them
key_points: New Zealand's Daryl Mitchell is the world's No.1-ranked ODI batter (802 rating points)
India's Shubman Gill is 2nd, just 1 point behind, on 801
Virat Kohli is 3rd (767) and Rohit Sharma 4th (758) — Rohit's 138 at Lord's cut his gap to Kohli to just 9 points
England's Joe Root jumped back into the world top 10 for the first time in 5 years after a strong series against India
stories.background: The ICC's ODI batting rankings score players on a rolling ratings system that weighs recent performances and the strength of opposition, not just career runs — so a single great innings moves the numbers but rarely leapfrogs a big gap on its own. This update follows the recently concluded India-England ODI series.
stories.what_happened: The ICC issued its latest Men's ODI batting rankings update: Mitchell holds top spot just ahead of Gill, with Kohli and Rohit close behind in 3rd and 4th, and Root re-entering the top 10 on the back of three fifty-plus scores in the series.
stories.why_it_matters: It's the tightest four-way race for the world's No.1 ODI batting spot in years, adding a fresh sub-plot to India's white-ball season regardless of match results.
stories.ripple_effects: Rankings feed into player reputations, sponsor value, and sometimes selection conversations — a top ranking is a marketable credential even outside of results on the field.
stories.why_now: The update follows directly from the just-finished India-England ODI series, where all four players' rating points shifted based on their performances.
stories.watch_next: Whether Gill or Mitchell holds on to the No.1 spot through India's next ODI assignment.

key_terms.definition: A rolling points system that ranks ODI batters by recent form and opposition strength, updated after most international series — not simply by total runs scored.

stories.the_lesson: Rankings reward consistency across a rating window, not a single big score — that's why one great innings only closes a gap, it doesn't leapfrog it outright.

stories.title: India hand a 150kmph pacer his T20I debut in Zimbabwe, as Iyer chases his first win in eight tries as captain
key_points: Shreyas Iyer won the toss and chose to bowl first in the series opener at Harare
India gave uncapped Gujarat Titans quick Ashok Sharma his international debut — he regularly bowls over 150kmph
It's Iyer's 8th match in charge without a win since taking over as India's T20I captain
India are the reigning T20 World Cup champions, yet arrived in Zimbabwe on cricket's longest active T20I losing streak
stories.background: A T20I debut is a player's first appearance for the national team in the format. Picking an uncapped, express-pace bowler is often a sign a team is actively searching for something different after a rough run — and India's rough run here is a genuinely unusual one, given they're the reigning world champions.
stories.what_happened: In the first T20I of India's tour of Zimbabwe, captain Shreyas Iyer won the toss and elected to field, and India handed a debut cap to fast bowler Ashok Sharma, known for regularly touching speeds above 150kmph (about 93mph) — among the fastest in world cricket.
stories.why_it_matters: It shows India's team management is willing to experiment with personnel to break a genuinely strange slump — a T20 World Cup-winning side that has gone winless in eight games since Iyer took the captaincy.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong debut could fast-track Sharma into India's white-ball pace plans ahead of bigger assignments; a continued losing streak would intensify scrutiny of Iyer's captaincy specifically.
stories.why_now: It's the first match of a fresh bilateral series, giving the team management a clean slate to try something new.
stories.watch_next: Whether Sharma's pace translates into wickets, and whether Iyer finally gets his first win as captain.

key_terms.definition: A Twenty20 International — the shortest official international cricket format, with each side batting for a maximum of 20 overs.

stories.the_lesson: When a team keeps losing despite being champions on paper, it usually reaches for a genuine wildcard — like raw, uncapped pace — rather than just repeating the same approach.

stories.title: Lalit Modi wins his 16-year IPL legal battle — and plans to finally return to India
key_points: A SAFEMA appellate tribunal set aside most penalties against Modi from the 2009 IPL-in-South-Africa case
The tribunal ruled the disputed foreign transfers were routine payments, not the illegal capital transfers he was accused of
Modi says he's 'looking forward to' returning to India after 16 years of self-exile in London
He was the IPL's founding chairman before being banned for life by the BCCI in 2010
stories.background: Lalit Modi founded and ran the Indian Premier League (IPL) — cricket's biggest T20 league — from 2008 until he was suspended and later banned for life by the BCCI (India's cricket board) in 2010, amid allegations of financial irregularities. One case centered on the 2009 IPL season, which had to be moved out of India to South Africa because of security concerns around that year's general election; the government's Enforcement Directorate alleged the BCCI sent nearly $50 million overseas for that tournament without the required central bank approval, a violation of India's foreign exchange law (FEMA). Modi has lived in London ever since, fighting the case.
stories.what_happened: The Appellate Tribunal under SAFEMA (the law governing forfeiture of illegally-acquired foreign exchange property) ruled that the foreign payments related to IPL 2009 were ordinary 'current account' transactions — routine payments for services — rather than the restricted 'capital account' transfers the Enforcement Directorate had alleged. The tribunal also found Modi personally wasn't responsible for the BCCI's compliance obligations, and set aside the penalties against him. Modi, in a statement from London, said he's now looking at returning to India for the first time in 16 years.
stories.why_it_matters: This closes one of Indian cricket administration's longest-running legal sagas and is a major personal vindication for the man who effectively invented the IPL's business model — the format that transformed cricket's economics globally. It also raises questions about whether Modi, once cricket's most powerful administrator, could re-enter Indian cricket or business circles.
stories.ripple_effects: A return could reignite old rivalries within the BCCI's power structure, where several current officials clashed with Modi during his tenure. It may also renew scrutiny of how the IPL's early finances were structured, even though the legal case against Modi himself has now largely collapsed.
stories.why_now: The tribunal delivered its judgment on July 16, and Modi's public statement confirming his plans to return came this week.
stories.watch_next: Whether Modi actually travels to India in the coming months, and how the BCCI — which separately banned him for life — responds.

key_terms.definition: India's law governing how money moves in and out of the country — violations can trigger asset seizures and penalties from the Enforcement Directorate.

stories.the_lesson: Regulatory cases built on complex financial technicalities — like whether a payment counts as a 'current' or 'capital' transaction — can take over a decade to resolve, and the underlying facts can look very different once a tribunal actually examines them in detail.

stories.title: Afghanistan names Rahmat Shah as new ODI and Test captain after Hashmatullah Shahidi steps down
key_points: Rahmat Shah named Afghanistan's new ODI and Test captain
Replaces Hashmatullah Shahidi, who stepped down
Rahmanullah Gurbaz named vice-captain in both formats
First assignment: ODI series vs Ireland from August 5
stories.background: Afghanistan's cricket team has become one of the most competitive sides in the world even amid turbulent leadership changes, highlighted by a memorable run to the Super Eight/knockout stage of the 2023 ODI World Cup. Hashmatullah Shahidi led the side through that stretch, with Rahmat Shah serving as his vice-captain.
stories.what_happened: The Afghanistan Cricket Board named Rahmat Shah as the new ODI and Test captain, replacing Hashmatullah Shahidi, who announced his decision to step down on social media. Wicketkeeper-batter Rahmanullah Gurbaz was named vice-captain across both formats. Rahmat, who debuted internationally in 2013 and has played 128 ODIs and 12 Tests, takes over immediately, with his first assignment being an ODI series against Ireland starting August 5.
stories.why_it_matters: Promoting the sitting vice-captain rather than bringing in an outsider is how cricket boards usually try to protect continuity through a leadership change — the aim is a smooth handover rather than a rebuild. It also signals confidence in Rahmat's experience for a team with real ambitions heading toward the next ODI World Cup cycle.
stories.ripple_effects: The change reshapes Afghanistan's leadership group heading into a busy stretch of white-ball cricket, and puts Gurbaz — a destructive, high-profile keeper-batter — into a groomed succession spot for the captaincy down the line.
stories.why_now: Shahidi's resignation and the board's swift naming of a successor suggest a planned, negotiated transition rather than a sudden falling-out, timed to land before the Ireland series in August.
stories.watch_next: How Rahmat Shah's Afghanistan perform in the August 5 ODI series against Ireland — his first assignment as full-time captain.

key_terms.definition: The player designated to lead the team if the captain is unavailable, injured, or rested, and who is often being groomed as the next full-time captain.

stories.the_lesson: Handing the captaincy to the experienced vice-captain instead of a wildcard pick is how teams try to protect continuity during a leadership change — the goal is to change the person in charge without changing how the team operates.

stories.title: BCCI bans no-ball offenders for the rest of the match and axes forfeiture in new domestic playing rules
key_points: A bowler ruled to have deliberately bowled a no-ball is now suspended for the rest of the match, not just the innings
Teams can no longer declare or forfeit an innings to manufacture a red-ball result
Wicketkeepers only need gloves behind the stumps at the moment of delivery, not through the run-up
New rules apply from next month's Duleep Trophy, the start of the 2026-27 domestic season
stories.background: Domestic cricket boards set their own playing conditions and often trial changes before they're proposed as international law through the MCC, cricket's rule-making body. India's BCCI runs the world's largest domestic cricket program, and its rule changes often shape the global conversation about the sport's laws.
stories.what_happened: The BCCI announced playing-condition changes for India's 2026-27 domestic season. The headline change: a bowler found by umpires to have deliberately bowled a no-ball — overstepping or not releasing the ball properly — is now suspended for the remainder of the match, up from just the rest of that innings. The board also scrapped the option for teams to declare or forfeit the final innings of a red-ball match, redefined what counts as an 'overthrow,' and eased wicketkeeper glove-positioning rules.
stories.why_it_matters: These are integrity fixes: deliberate no-balls have been used to manipulate over rates or run-outs, and forfeiture rules have occasionally been used to engineer contrived run-chases or results. Tightening both closes loopholes in India's high-stakes domestic system, which feeds directly into the national team and the IPL talent pipeline.
stories.ripple_effects: Domestic bowlers and captains across the Ranji Trophy and other first-class competitions must adjust tactics immediately, since the new rules start with next month's Duleep Trophy. Because BCCI rule changes often get picked up by the MCC and other boards, this could eventually influence international playing conditions too.
stories.why_now: The changes were announced just ahead of the new domestic season, which begins with the Duleep Trophy in August.
stories.watch_next: Whether any bowler is actually suspended under the new deliberate no-ball rule once the Duleep Trophy gets underway.

key_terms.definition: Extra runs scored when a fielder's throw at the stumps misses and goes to the boundary or is fumbled — the new rule defines it only as a throw specifically aimed at breaking the stumps or preventing a run.

stories.the_lesson: When a competition's incentives create room for gaming the rules — like using a technical foul to manage an over or an innings — regulators eventually have to raise the cost of that behavior rather than just condemn it.

stories.title: Sri Lanka's Kusal Mendis ruled out with a hamstring injury, weeks before his side's Test series against India
key_points: Mendis out of LPL 2026 with a hamstring injury
Doubt now hangs over Sri Lanka's Test series vs India
That series starts August 15
Blow to both his franchise, Colombo Kaps, and the national side
stories.background: Kusal Mendis is one of Sri Lanka's key senior batters across formats and a regular feature of the national Test side. The Lanka Premier League (LPL) is Sri Lanka's domestic T20 franchise tournament, which players use partly as match fitness and form practice in the lead-up to bigger international assignments — in this case, a home Test series against India starting August 15.
stories.what_happened: Mendis has sustained a hamstring injury and been ruled out of the rest of LPL 2026, playing for Colombo Kaps. The injury raises doubts about his fitness and availability for Sri Lanka's upcoming two-Test home series against India, which begins August 15.
stories.why_it_matters: Losing a senior batter's form and match practice this close to a marquee series against a top opponent like India weakens Sri Lanka's preparation at a sensitive time, and puts more selection pressure on the team management to find cover.
stories.ripple_effects: If Mendis isn't fit in time, Sri Lanka will need to promote a replacement batter into the Test squad on short notice, which usually means a less experienced player facing India's strong bowling attack under pressure.
stories.why_now: The injury happened during ongoing LPL 2026 matches, with the India series only about three weeks away.
stories.watch_next: Mendis's recovery timeline and whether he's declared fit before the first Test on August 15.

key_terms.definition: A strain or tear in the muscles at the back of the thigh — one of the most common injuries in cricket, often needing several weeks of rest to heal fully.

stories.the_lesson: In international sport, injuries picked up in domestic warm-up tournaments can end up mattering more than the tournament itself, because they ripple straight into a team's readiness for the marquee series that follows.

stories.title: Shreyas Iyer chases his first win as India T20I captain against Zimbabwe, after seven straight defeats in charge
key_points: 1st T20I today at Harare Sports Club, 4:30pm IST
Iyer is winless in seven matches as India's T20I captain
India lost series in both Ireland and England recently
Squad features four uncapped, largely young players
stories.background: Shreyas Iyer was handed India's T20I captaincy as part of a broader generational transition after senior players stepped back from the format. Since taking over, the results haven't gone his way — India has lost series in Ireland (2-0) and then in England (4-0), leaving Iyer under real pressure heading into this tour, even though the current squad is deliberately youth-heavy and experimental.
stories.what_happened: India begins a three-match T20I series against Zimbabwe at the Harare Sports Club today, with Shreyas Iyer captaining a young, largely inexperienced squad that includes four uncapped players. Iyer has lost all seven matches he's captained so far and is seeking his first win in the role. Zimbabwe, led by Sikandar Raza, will look to capitalise on India fielding a second-string side.
stories.why_it_matters: This series is as much a test of India's next generation of white-ball talent as it is about the result — selectors are watching how these young players perform under a captain who himself needs a confidence-building win. A third straight series defeat would intensify questions about Iyer's captaincy just as India looks toward the next T20 World Cup cycle.
stories.ripple_effects: Poor results here could accelerate calls for a captaincy change or a return to more experienced players for the format; strong performances by the uncapped players could fast-track their path into India's first-choice T20I XI.
stories.why_now: The first match of the series is being played today.
stories.watch_next: Whether Iyer's India can convert an easier opponent (Zimbabwe, generally ranked below India) into his first win as captain.

key_terms.definition: A Twenty20 International — the shortest format of international cricket, with each team batting for a maximum of 20 overs.

stories.the_lesson: A new captain's early results are often less about their leadership and more about the team's transition phase — judging captaincy fairly usually requires looking past a small early sample of results.

stories.title: Pakistan's women start a 3-match ODI series in Sri Lanka today — against a hot Sri Lankan side
key_points: Sri Lanka Women vs Pakistan Women, 1st ODI, in Hambantota today
3-match series runs July 23, 25 and 28, all at the same venue
Sri Lanka arrive fresh off a 2-1 ODI series win away in Bangladesh
Sri Lanka captained by veteran Chamari Athapaththu
stories.background: Women's cricket has been getting a steadily bigger international calendar in recent years, with more bilateral series between full-member cricket nations. Sri Lanka and Pakistan are both mid-tier teams in the women's game, building toward future ICC events, and series like this matter for team rankings and player development even when they don't get the attention men's cricket does.
stories.what_happened: Sri Lanka Women host Pakistan Women in the first of three ODIs at the Mahinda Rajapaksa International Cricket Stadium in Hambantota, starting today. All three matches (July 23, 25, 28) are being played at the same venue. Sri Lanka, led by Chamari Athapaththu, come into the series having just won an away ODI series against Bangladesh 2-1.
stories.why_it_matters: For both teams, this series is a form and ranking opportunity ahead of the next ICC Women's events — good results here feed into qualification and seeding for future tournaments, and give players a chance to cement their spots in the XI.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong series for either side shifts momentum into their next assignments and affects selection conversations for upcoming ICC tournament squads. It also adds to Sri Lanka's recent good run of form across their women's team, following the Bangladesh series win.
stories.why_now: This is the opening match of the series, played today.
stories.watch_next: The result of today's 1st ODI, and whether Sri Lanka can extend their winning momentum from the Bangladesh series against a full-strength Pakistan side.

key_terms.definition: A cricket match format where each team bats for a maximum of 50 overs — shorter than a multi-day Test match, longer than a T20.

stories.the_lesson: Momentum in sport often carries across series and formats — a team coming off a series win, like Sri Lanka here, typically starts the next assignment with more confidence and clearer roles than one starting fresh.

stories.title: India carry cricket's longest active T20I winless streak into today's series opener in Zimbabwe
key_points: India play Zimbabwe's 1st T20I today in Harare at 4:30pm IST
India, the reigning T20 World Cup champions, are on their longest-ever winless run in the format
Captain Shreyas Iyer has yet to win a single match since taking over — 7 straight defeats
Zimbabwe have historically only beaten India at this exact venue, Harare Sports Club
stories.background: India won the T20 World Cup earlier this year but have since gone into an unprecedented slump in the format under new white-ball captain Shreyas Iyer, who took over as part of a leadership transition. Ahead of this Zimbabwe tour, injuries to spinner Varun Chakravarthy and pacer Harshit Rana forced India to call up uncapped players Ravi Bishnoi and Prince Yadav, adding inexperience to an already out-of-sorts squad (as we reported earlier this week).
stories.what_happened: India play the first of three T20Is against Zimbabwe today at Harare Sports Club. Coming in, India — the reigning world champions — are in the middle of their longest-ever winless streak in T20I history, and Iyer is winless in seven matches since becoming captain. Historically, India lead the head-to-head 11-3, but all three of Zimbabwe's wins have come at this exact ground, making today's venue a genuine banana skin.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a rare and uncomfortable position for the reigning world champions to be defending against a lower-ranked side, and it puts real pressure on a new captain in only his first assignment. A result today either starts to turn the slump around or deepens questions about the team's form heading into a busier international calendar.
stories.ripple_effects: A continued losing streak would intensify media and selector scrutiny on Iyer's captaincy and the team's transition away from its World-Cup-winning core, while a win would relieve pressure ahead of tougher assignments. It also gives debut opportunities — like uncapped call-ups Bishnoi and Prince Yadav — a higher-stakes stage to make an impression.
stories.why_now: Today's match is the direct continuation of the story we flagged earlier this week: India arriving in Zimbabwe winless and searching for a fix, with the series opener now the first real test of whether the reshuffled squad can turn results around.
stories.watch_next: Today's result, and whether the uncapped players called up for the injured Chakravarthy and Rana get a game.

key_terms.definition: The overall win-loss tally between two teams across their entire history of matches against each other.

stories.the_lesson: Even the best teams can hit a genuine form slump — reputation and past results don't guarantee current performance, which is exactly why every match still has to be played.

thread.previously: We reported India calling up uncapped players Ravi Bishnoi and Prince Yadav after injuries to Varun Chakravarthy and Harshit Rana, as India arrived in Zimbabwe on cricket's longest active T20I winless streak.
thread.changed: The series opener is now underway today, the first real test of whether the reshuffled, injury-hit squad can end the losing run.

prev_ref.title: India call up Ravi Bishnoi and Prince Yadav after Varun Chakravarthy and Harshit Rana are ruled out injured

stories.title: Nepal's World Cup qualification hopes are on the line as they face the Netherlands in a must-win tri-series match
key_points: Netherlands host Nepal today in Utrecht in the ICC Men's Cricket World Cup League 2
Nepal sit 5th in the League 2 table with 28 points from 28 matches — outside the direct-qualifying top 4
Namibia, Nepal and Netherlands are playing a tri-series through July 31 for crucial ranking points
Top 4 finishers in League 2 qualify directly for the 2027 World Cup Qualifier
stories.background: The ICC Cricket World Cup League 2 is a multi-year one-day-international league where lower-ranked cricket nations (like Nepal, Namibia, Oman, and the USA) play each other over dozens of matches, with the top finishers earning a route toward qualifying for the 50-over Cricket World Cup — a huge deal for teams outside cricket's traditional 'Full Member' elite, who otherwise rarely get ODI status matches at all. Nepal has a passionate, fast-growing cricket following and has been pushing for years to break into the sport's top tier.
stories.what_happened: Netherlands and Nepal play each other today, July 23, in Utrecht as part of a tri-series with Namibia running from July 21 to 31, 2026, part of the ongoing League 2 competition. Nepal currently sit fifth in the League 2 standings with 28 points from 28 matches played — just outside the top four required for direct qualification to the 2027 World Cup Qualifier.
stories.why_it_matters: For a team like Nepal, every one of these matches is close to must-win: missing the top four means dropping into a lower-probability qualification path, or missing the World Cup picture altogether. These matches get a fraction of the attention of India or England fixtures, but they decide which nations get a shot at cricket's biggest stage.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong showing raises Nepal's global cricket profile and can boost sponsorship, board revenue and youth participation at home. A poor one narrows their qualification path significantly with fewer League 2 matches left to make up ground.
stories.why_now: Today's fixture is part of a tightly packed, points-defining stretch for all three teams before the League 2 window closes.
stories.watch_next: The final League 2 standings once this tri-series and remaining fixtures conclude, and whether Nepal climbs into the top four.

key_terms.definition: Official recognition from the ICC that a match counts as a full One Day International — which affects team rankings and player records, and is not automatically given to every cricket-playing nation.

stories.the_lesson: In global sport, the matches that decide who even gets to compete at the top level often happen far from the spotlight — the real competition for many nations is qualifying, not winning it all.

stories.title: The Global Super League kicks off in Guyana today — a $1 million showdown between the world's franchise-league champions
key_points: Five teams from five different T20 leagues compete for a $1 million prize pool
Lahore Qalandars (PSL) face Perth Scorchers XI (BBL) in today's opener
San Francisco Unicorns represent US franchise cricket alongside Major League Cricket's rise
Tournament runs July 23 to August 1 at Guyana's Providence stadium
stories.background: The Global Super League, organized by Cricket West Indies, pits the champion or representative teams of major T20 franchise leagues against each other: the Caribbean Premier League (CPL), Pakistan Super League (PSL), Big Bash League (BBL), International League T20 (ILT20), and now Major League Cricket (MLC) from the US. It's now in its third edition, and this year's field includes defending champions Guyana Amazon Warriors, Desert Vipers, Lahore Qalandars, Perth Scorchers XI, and San Francisco Unicorns.
stories.what_happened: The 2026 Global Super League begins today at the Guyana National Stadium in Providence, with Lahore Qalandars facing Perth Scorchers XI in the opening match. Five teams will compete over ten days for a $1 million prize pool, with San Francisco Unicorns making their tournament debut as Major League Cricket's representative — a sign of US franchise cricket's growing standing on the world stage.
stories.why_it_matters: A 'champions league' format that pits winners of different national T20 leagues against each other is still a relatively new idea in cricket, and its growth — now including a US team — reflects how fast T20 franchise cricket is expanding globally, with money and player interest following.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong showing from San Francisco Unicorns would boost the credibility of US franchise cricket and Major League Cricket's push to attract more international stars. For players, the tournament is also a showcase that can influence which leagues bid for them in future auctions.
stories.why_now: The tournament's fixed July slot fits between the end of several domestic T20 seasons and the start of international commitments, giving top franchise players a short window to compete before returning to national duty.
stories.watch_next: Watch how San Francisco Unicorns perform against more established franchise sides — a competitive showing would be a meaningful marker for the credibility of US cricket.

key_terms.definition: A domestic T20 cricket competition where privately owned teams (not national sides) sign players through an auction or draft, like the IPL, PSL or Big Bash.

stories.the_lesson: When a sport creates a 'best of the best leagues' tournament, it's usually a sign the underlying domestic competitions have grown strong and lucrative enough to be worth cross-pollinating — it's a symptom of a sport's economic growth, not just a scheduling gimmick.

stories.title: India's under-19s hold on for a draw as Sri Lanka survive a tense final day in Colombo
key_points: The 2nd Youth Test ended in a draw at the R Premadasa Stadium
India posted a huge 411 in the first innings, then declared their second innings
Sri Lanka's under-19s batted out the final day to save the match
The two-match series is now level after a draw in the first Test too
stories.background: India's under-19 team has been touring Sri Lanka for a two-match Youth Test series — the junior version of a Test match, used to develop future international players. India piled up a commanding first-innings total of 411 thanks to a 122-run partnership between Kushagra Ojha and captain Yashbardhan Chauhan, putting Sri Lanka's under-19s under serious pressure to save the game.
stories.what_happened: After India declared their second innings on 208 for 3, Sri Lanka's under-19s were left needing to survive the rest of the match without losing all their wickets. They did exactly that, batting out the final day to end the match in a draw. The result means the two-match series finishes level, since the first Youth Test between the sides also ended in a draw, with India's Lakshay Raichandani scoring a double century in that game.
stories.why_it_matters: For young players, learning to bat out a match under pressure — rather than chasing a result — is one of the core skills that separates Test-quality batters from limited-overs specialists, and Sri Lanka's under-19s showed they could do it here.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong performances in this series, including Raichandani's double century and Ojha's first-innings knock, will factor into India's next under-19 World Cup selection conversations. For Sri Lanka, successfully saving both Tests is an encouraging sign for a young batting group.
stories.why_now: The match reached its natural conclusion today, the fourth and final scheduled day of the Test.
stories.watch_next: Watch for how these young players are used in India and Sri Lanka's senior domestic setups over the coming season, as a first step toward international selection.

key_terms.definition: A multi-day cricket match played in Test-match format by under-19 teams, used to prepare young players for senior first-class and international cricket.

stories.the_lesson: In the longest form of cricket, surviving with your wickets intact can be just as valuable a skill as scoring quickly — a draw earned by batting out the clock is still a genuine result, not a non-event.

thread.previously: India posted a first-innings 411, and Sri Lanka's under-19s were fighting to survive the rest of the match to avoid defeat.
thread.changed: Sri Lanka's under-19s successfully batted out the final day, and the match ended in a draw, meaning the two-match series finishes level at 0-0.

prev_ref.title: Sri Lanka's under-19s fight to save the Youth Test in Colombo after India's first-innings 411

stories.title: Namibia Women win a Super Over thriller against Uganda in a T20 Quadrangular Series tie
key_points: Namibia and Uganda both scored 123/7 — match finished tied after 20 overs each
Namibia won the Super Over (cricket's tie-breaker) to take the match
In the day's other fixture, Hong Kong Women beat Tanzania Women by 16 runs
Part of a T20 Quadrangular Series being hosted in Namibia
stories.background: A Quadrangular Series is a tournament between four national teams, each playing the others in a round-robin. This one, hosted in Namibia, features the Namibia, Uganda, Hong Kong and Tanzania women's teams — nations that don't get frequent international fixtures, making tournaments like this important for their players' match experience and world rankings.
stories.what_happened: Namibia Women and Uganda Women finished tied on 123 for 7 apiece after their full 20 overs, sending the match to a Super Over — cricket's version of a shootout, where each team faces one extra over to settle the result. Namibia won the Super Over to take the match. In the tournament's other fixture that day, Hong Kong Women beat Tanzania Women by 16 runs after Tanzania set a target of 136.
stories.why_it_matters: Tied matches decided by Super Overs are rare and high-tension; for associate/emerging cricket nations like Namibia and Uganda, results like this carry outsized importance for team morale, ICC rankings, and funding, since these teams get far fewer chances to play international cricket than major nations.
stories.ripple_effects: A Super Over win for Namibia boosts their standing in this tournament's table and gives their players rare high-pressure match experience that can translate to future ICC qualifying events. It also raises Uganda's profile despite the loss, having pushed a full match to the very last over.
stories.why_now: This was part of the ongoing Quadrangular Series schedule in Namibia, with matches being played across the tournament's group stage this week.
stories.watch_next: Watch the remaining Quadrangular Series fixtures to see which two teams advance furthest, and whether this result affects Namibia and Uganda's positions in ICC Women's T20I rankings.
stories.the_lesson: In limited-overs cricket, a tie doesn't end the contest — a Super Over exists precisely because sport often needs a designed tie-breaker to produce a winner, a concept that shows up far beyond cricket (penalty shootouts, overtime periods, runoff elections).

stories.title: Phil Salt stars as Welsh Fire beat Southern Brave in a tense men's Hundred finish
key_points: Welsh Fire chased down Southern Brave's 135/7, winning by 6 wickets with 2 balls to spare
Phil Salt named Player of the Match for his knock at the top of the order
Southern Brave's women's team won their own match earlier the same day
Both teams' games are part of The Hundred, England's short-format summer competition
stories.background: The Hundred is an English cricket competition using a shortened '100-ball' format (instead of the usual 120-ball T20), designed to be quicker and more accessible for new fans. Each men's and women's team plays a full round of fixtures over the English summer, and results in one gender's match are entirely separate from the other's, even on the same match day.
stories.what_happened: In the men's Hundred fixture at Southampton, Southern Brave posted 135 for 7. Welsh Fire chased it down for the loss of only 4 wickets, getting home with just 2 balls to spare in their 100-ball innings. Phil Salt was named Player of the Match for his innings at the top of Welsh Fire's order.
stories.why_it_matters: Tight, low-scoring run-chases like this are exactly the close finishes The Hundred was designed to produce — good viewing for the competition, and a strong personal result for Salt, an England white-ball regular, ahead of the international calendar resuming later in the year.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong individual score from an established international player like Salt tends to reinforce selectors' confidence heading into future England white-ball squads, while Welsh Fire's win moves them up (and Southern Brave's men's side down) in this year's Hundred table.
stories.why_now: This was simply the scheduled Match 2 of the men's competition for both teams this season.
stories.watch_next: Watch the points table as both teams' campaigns continue through The Hundred's group stage over the coming weeks.
stories.the_lesson: In shortened cricket formats, even a small buffer of balls remaining (here, 2 balls to spare) reflects how tight margins are engineered into the format on purpose — it's built for tension, not just speed.

stories.title: Sri Lanka's under-19s fight to save the Youth Test in Colombo after India's first-innings 411
key_points: Sri Lanka U19, following on after India's 411, reached 155/4 in their second innings by day 3
Vimath Dinsara battled to an unbeaten 55 to anchor the rebuild
It's a bounce-back from Sri Lanka being reduced to 122/8 the day before
The 4-day Youth Test in Colombo runs July 20–23

thread.previously: India's Under-19s had piled up 411 in their first innings, with centuries from Kushagra Ojha and Manal Chauhan, and had reduced Sri Lanka's Under-19s to a struggling 122/8 in reply.
thread.changed: Sri Lanka's first innings folded well behind India's total, forcing them to follow on. By the close of day 3, in their second innings, Sri Lanka had rebuilt to 155 for 4, with Vimath Dinsara unbeaten on 55, fighting to save the match from an innings defeat.

stories.background: This is the second Youth Test between the India and Sri Lanka Under-19 teams, part of a tour that also included a multi-day series and is being used to develop the next generation of players for both boards.
stories.what_happened: By the close of play on day 3 of the four-day Youth Test in Colombo (July 20–23), Sri Lanka's Under-19s were batting again — this time in their second innings, having been made to follow on after India's first-innings 411. Vimath Dinsara held firm on 55 not out as Sri Lanka reached 155 for 4, trying to bat out the match and avoid an innings defeat.
stories.why_it_matters: It's the key passage of play deciding whether India's under-19 side completes a dominant innings win or Sri Lanka claws its way to a draw.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong finish either way feeds into both boards' assessment of their next generation of Test players.
stories.why_now: Day 3 play concluded today, July 22, with one day of the match remaining.
stories.watch_next: The result on the final day, July 23 — whether Sri Lanka can bat out the match or India completes an innings win.

key_terms.definition: In multi-day cricket, if a team falls far enough behind after the first innings, the leading team can force them to bat again immediately rather than batting again itself.

stories.the_lesson: In multi-day cricket, a big first-innings deficit doesn't end a team's chances — a resilient second-innings partnership can still salvage a draw.

prev_ref.title: India's Under-19s pile up 411 and reduce Sri Lanka to 122/8 — young batters Kushagra Ojha and Manal Chauhan both score centuries

stories.title: Pakistan's under-19 women level their T20 series with South Africa after a nervy 6-wicket win
key_points: Pakistan Women U19 beat South Africa Women U19 by 6 wickets in the 4th T20I in Karachi
South Africa posted 109/8; Zoofishan Ayyaz's unbeaten 44 guided Pakistan's chase
The 5-match series is now level at 2-2 with one match left
Series has swung back and forth — South Africa won games 1 and 3, Pakistan games 2 and 4
stories.background: Pakistan and South Africa's under-19 women's teams are playing a five-match T20I series at the National Bank Stadium in Karachi, running July 14–24, 2026 — the kind of age-group series both boards use to spot and develop future senior internationals.
stories.what_happened: At the National Bank Stadium in Karachi on July 22, South Africa Women U19 were restricted to 109 for 8 in their 20 overs. Pakistan Women U19 chased the target down with six wickets in hand, powered by Zoofishan Ayyaz's unbeaten 44, to level the five-match series 2-2 ahead of the decider.
stories.why_it_matters: It sets up a series decider and keeps both age-group programs' bragging rights alive heading into the final match.
stories.ripple_effects: Selectors on both sides will be watching individual performances like Ayyaz's for future senior-team selection.
stories.why_now: The match was played today, July 22.
stories.watch_next: The deciding 5th T20I, scheduled for July 24 at the same venue.
stories.the_lesson: Youth international series like this one are where future senior players first get noticed by selectors.

stories.title: India call up Ravi Bishnoi and Prince Yadav after Varun Chakravarthy and Harshit Rana are ruled out injured
key_points: Leg-spinner Ravi Bishnoi and pacer Prince Yadav join India's Zimbabwe T20I squad
They replace Varun Chakravarthy (hamstring) and Harshit Rana, both injured in England
Chakravarthy heads to the BCCI's Centre of Excellence for rehab
Bishnoi struggled in his last England outing, conceding 60 runs in 4 overs
stories.background: India are touring Zimbabwe for a three-match T20I series starting July 23, led by Shreyas Iyer, just after wrapping up a difficult white-ball tour of England. Varun Chakravarthy had already missed India's last two T20Is in England with a hamstring injury.
stories.what_happened: The BCCI (India's cricket board) confirmed Chakravarthy needs further recovery time and will report to its Centre of Excellence in Bengaluru for rehabilitation, while pacer Harshit Rana was also ruled out. Leg-spinner Ravi Bishnoi and seamer Prince Yadav have been added to the squad for the Zimbabwe series as their replacements. Bishnoi featured in England's first two T20Is but had a rough outing in the second match at Old Trafford, going for 60 runs in his four overs.
stories.why_it_matters: India's spin-bowling and pace-bowling depth is being tested in real time — losing two bowlers to injury mid-tour forces the team management to lean on reserves just as India try to end their longest-ever T20I winless streak, which stands at eight matches since winning the T20 World Cup.
stories.ripple_effects: Bishnoi, who had a difficult recent outing, now gets another chance to prove himself in India's playing XI, while Chakravarthy's recovery timeline becomes a fitness question mark ahead of India's busier white-ball calendar later in the year.
stories.why_now: The squad update comes just a day before the series opener on July 23 at Harare Sports Club, giving the new call-ups almost no lead-in time before a potential debut or recall.
stories.watch_next: Whether Bishnoi or Prince Yadav make India's playing XI for the July 23 series opener, and Chakravarthy's recovery update from the BCCI's Centre of Excellence.
stories.the_lesson: Squad depth matters as much as star power — a team's ability to absorb injuries mid-tour without collapsing often decides results as much as its first-choice XI does.

stories.title: West Indies coach Daren Sammy backs injured captain Roston Chase to be fit for the first Pakistan Test
key_points: Chase has a finger injury that ruled him out of the recent New Zealand ODI series
Head coach Daren Sammy says he's confident Chase will recover in time
First Test against Pakistan starts July 25 in Trinidad, with valuable World Test Championship points on the line
Chase is both West Indies' captain and their main spin-bowling all-rounder, making his fitness a big swing factor
stories.background: Roston Chase captains West Indies in Test cricket and is also their frontline spin-bowling all-rounder, meaning his absence hurts the team twice over — in leadership and in the XI's balance. He sustained an injury to his right index finger that kept him out of West Indies' recently finished three-match ODI series against New Zealand. West Indies now host Pakistan for a two-match Test series that counts toward the World Test Championship (WTC) — a league table run over a two-year cycle where results feed into rankings and eventually a final, and where both these sides currently sit near the bottom.
stories.what_happened: Head coach Daren Sammy said he's confident Chase will recover in time for the series-opening Test against Pakistan, which starts July 25 in Trinidad. Chase missed the New Zealand ODIs with swelling in the injured finger, but Sammy said the team's medical staff has a plan to keep him on the field, calling Chase 'a fighter' who won't want to miss Test cricket.
stories.why_it_matters: With both West Indies and Pakistan struggling near the bottom of the WTC standings, this series is a rare chance for either side to climb the table, and Chase's fitness directly affects West Indies' chances — losing their captain and a key all-rounder on the eve of the series would be a serious blow to a side already fighting to be competitive in Test cricket.
stories.ripple_effects: If Chase can't play, West Indies would need to hand the captaincy to a stand-in and rebalance their bowling attack without a frontline spinner, likely weakening their chances in conditions that could suit Pakistan's more experienced Test squad, itself led by a returning Babar Azam.
stories.why_now: Sammy's comments came just three days before the first Test begins, as the fitness call on Chase needs to be finalized ahead of the squad selection.
stories.watch_next: Watch for West Indies' final team confirmation and toss news on July 25 to see whether Chase actually takes the field.

key_terms.definition: A league table that ranks Test-playing nations over a roughly two-year cycle based on match results, with the top two meeting in a final.

stories.the_lesson: In cricket, a single player who combines two roles — like captain and frontline spinner — creates a single point of failure: their fitness alone can swing a team's balance far more than an ordinary player's would.

stories.title: Red Bull turns down a $3 billion offer for its junior F1 team, Racing Bulls
key_points: Abu Dhabi-backed investors offered around $3 billion for Racing Bulls
Red Bull said no, signaling it's staying committed to F1 long-term
It's reportedly at least the second rejected bid — an earlier one valued the team near $2-2.3bn
Neither side has officially confirmed the approach or the rejection
stories.background: Racing Bulls (formerly Toro Rosso, then AlphaTauri) is Red Bull's second F1 team, based in Faenza, Italy — originally bought to give young drivers a place to prove themselves before a possible promotion to the main Red Bull team. F1 teams have become dramatically more valuable in recent years as the sport's global TV and sponsorship revenue has boomed, turning even mid-grid teams into billion-dollar assets that outside investors now want a piece of.
stories.what_happened: Red Bull turned down a roughly $3 billion bid to buy Racing Bulls, according to multiple reports. The bid was backed by Abu Dhabi-based investment funds and fronted by financier Andrew Harriott and Dean Attew, a former associate of ex-F1 boss Bernie Ecclestone. It follows an earlier approach that reportedly valued the team lower, around $2-2.3 billion, which Red Bull also rejected. Neither Red Bull nor the reported bidders have publicly confirmed the approach, so the deal and its rejection remain unconfirmed by the parties directly involved.
stories.why_it_matters: The size of the rejected offer is itself the story — it shows just how much F1 team valuations have inflated, and Red Bull turning down billions signals the junior team is worth more to them as a development pipeline (and as leverage in driver contracts) than as a cash payout.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect more speculative bids for F1 teams as outside capital — sovereign wealth funds, private equity — keeps chasing the sport's growing commercial value; F1's other smaller teams could see similar unsolicited offers. It also matters for driver careers: as long as Red Bull keeps Racing Bulls, it keeps control of one of the sport's few reliable pathways for young drivers to reach a top team.
stories.why_now: The report surfaced this week as part of the broader Hungarian Grand Prix news cycle, alongside other Red Bull driver-market stories.
